focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 18

Three weeks after Storm Mouth took the dead, low water showed what was left to pay for.

The House of Oaths stood above Knot Bend on stripped pilings, half the old floor hacked out, the east wall leaning like a bad tooth. Mud had dried in a thick black cuff around every post. A goat skeleton hung under the landing where floodweed had wrapped it in place and then gone slack. The room that had once held Sereya's relic shelves still stank of soaked reed mat, lamp grease, and the sweet rot of timber that had taken water too long and never meant to survive it.

Riva stood on the slant of the landing roof with a pry bar under a length of warped plank and leaned until the nails screamed. When the board came loose, it let go all at once. She caught the far end against her thigh before it could slide and stove in the ladder below. The wood bent under her palm like wet bread. There was no patching that. It might make firewood, if it dried enough not to poison the smoke.

Below her, Daro gave two low beats on the frame drum he had taken to carrying under one arm. The sound came up through the stripped house and the open water under it, a round dull pulse that told the men on the mudbank to ease the replacement beam higher. He could still speak if he had to, but his voice had never come back from Storm Mouth as anything a crowd could stand under. When he forced it, the sound came out scraped raw, as if somebody had lined his throat with fishbone. So he kept time instead. Reedmouth had learned the beats fast. Two beats meant lift. Three meant hold. One hard strike meant somebody was about to lose a hand.

It made sense. Riva trusted a beat more than prayer.

She tossed the rotten plank down onto the salvage pile. Tovin Reed looked over from the sawhorses and snorted....
prev_tail_excerpt:
...Right now it was useful.

Bato looked from one sibling to the next and, for the first time since Iven had met him, showed something naked as grief.

"Jun was the sixth mouth because Sereya tried to pay with a child and a lover instead of with the work divided right," he said. "Don't do her trick again."

The sentence went through every one of them. You could see it land. In Riva as a tightening of stance. In Lale as fury made useful. In Nami as horror stripped of glamour. In Daro as a kind of collapsing refusal. In Iven as the final cheap angle leaving the room.

The full ruined set of them was all the ritual would get.

The rescued pilgrim rolled to his knees, coughing, and stared at Daro with river water pouring out of his beard. "If you can't do it alone," he said, missing tooth bright in the dark of his mouth, "say it before the rest of us drown proving you can't."

Daro shut his eyes.

When he opened them again, he did not pull the pole free yet. Pride could still kill half a district between one heartbeat and the next. But he looked at the four unopened housings, at Riva's key, at Lale's marked arm, at Nami with the guilty shape of the long-hidden salt-fig seed under the wet cloth at her pocket, at Iven half upright with his left hand turned into butchered rope-meat because the ferry had needed to swing, and something in his face stopped resembling inheritance.

Riva said, "All five. Now we know."

Iven leaned his shoulder against the post to keep the world from tipping out under him. The capstan sat three feet away, innocent as a stool, blood in its teeth where his hand had been. He stared at it because it was easier than staring at anybody who loved him in whatever broken family way they still knew how.

"Then move," he said. "Before the mouth takes more names."
